REFERENCE: The reference appears plausible with author names, title, and a general BMJ format. However, a critical inconsistency is the DOI: 10.1016/S0140-6736(10)61431-6. This DOI format (10.1016/S0140-6736) is characteristic of The Lancet, not the BMJ. BMJ DOIs typically start with 10.1136. The volume and page numbers (593(7857):136-141) do not match BMJ's standard 4-digit volume numbering. Also, the title describes a 2021 SARS-CoV-2 study, but the DOI pattern suggests a 2010 Lancet article, creating a temporal and publisher mismatch. Thus, the reference is likely fabricated.
authors: Authors: Collier DA, De Marco A, Ferreira IATM, Meng B, Datir RP, Walls AC, et al. All have plausible names for virology/immunology researchers. The 'et al.' is standard for multiple authors. No obvious fabrication detected.
title: Title: 'Sensitivity of SARS-CoV-2 B.1.1.7 to mRNA vaccine-elicited antibodies.' This is a plausible and well-formed title for a 2021 COVID-19 study. It matches real research topics. No fabrication detected in the title itself.
journal_name: The journal is cited as 'BMJ', which is the official abbreviation for the British Medical Journal. The name itself is correct, but the issue details (volume, pages) do not align with BMJ publications in 2021. The journal name is valid, but the context is wrong.
journal_volume: Volume: 593. BMJ volumes in 2021 were in the 370s to 380s (e.g., 372, 373, 374). Volume 593 is not a valid BMJ volume for that period. This is a major inconsistency. Also, the parenthetical (7857) suggests a specific issue number, but '593(7857)' is not a standard format (usually issue numbers are smaller integers). The issue number 7857 is unusually high and likely fake.
journal_issue: Journal: BMJ. Issue: 593(7857). BMJ volumes are typically 4-digit numbers (e.g., 372, 373). Volume 593 is inconsistent with BMJ's numbering (BMJ volumes range in the 300s in 2021). The page numbers 136-141 for volume 593 are also implausible. This does not match any real BMJ issue.
journal_page: Page numbers: 136-141. For a 2021 article in BMJ (or any major journal), page numbers of only 6 pages are possible but less common with modern elocators. However, the volume (593) and page range do not match any real BMJ article. Likely fabricated.
elocator: The reference uses page numbers 136-141, not a separate elocator like an article number or e-pages. For a 2021 BMJ article, modern references often use elocators (e.g., e123456). The absence of an elocator is unusual for recent high-impact journals, but not necessarily invalid. Given the overall fabrication, this is likely an error.
publication_date: Publication date: 2021 May. The reference includes 'BMJ. 2021 May;'. The month and year are provided. For a real article, a specific day is often included (e.g., 2021 May 13). The lack of a day is not necessarily invalid, but the volume/issue mismatch undermines the date. The date is plausible on its own, but the context makes it false.
doi: DOI: 10.1016/S0140-6736(10)61431-6. This DOI format is exclusively used by The Lancet (S0140-6736 series). The reference claims the article is in BMJ, which uses DOI prefixes like 10.1136. The number pattern (10)61431-6 suggests a 2010 publication, contradicting the 2021 date of the described study. This is a clear mismatch.
pmcid: No PMCID is provided in the reference. Not applicable.
pmid: No PMID is provided. Not applicable.

REFERENCE: The reference is a hallucinated mixture of a real Nature article's metadata with the journal name of BMJ and a completely unrelated Lancet DOI.
authors: The listed authors are actual researchers who collaborated on SARS-CoV-2 studies, but the author list is primarily associated with a paper in Nature, not BMJ.
title: The title is a real and highly cited article title, but it was published in Nature, not BMJ.
journal_name: The BMJ is a real medical journal, but it did not publish the article with this title or the specified volume/issue.
journal_volume: Volume 593 belongs to the journal Nature for the year 2021, and does not exist for the journal BMJ.
journal_issue: Issue 7857 is a Nature issue number from April 2021, and is not a valid issue number for the BMJ.
journal_page: Pages 136-141 in Nature Volume 593 correspond to this specific article title, but they are incorrectly attributed to BMJ.
elocator: The citation does not provide a specific e-locator string other than the DOI.
publication_date: The paper was published online in March and in print in April 2021; May 2021 is slightly incorrect but close.
doi: The DOI 10.1016/S0140-6736(10)61431-6 belongs to a 2010 Lancet article about the metal-on-metal hip resurfacing, not the SARS-CoV-2 paper listed.
pmcid: No PMCID was provided in the source string to validate.
pmid: No PubMed ID was provided in the source string to validate.

REFERENCE: This is clearly a corrupted or fabricated version of a real reference. The actual paper by Collier DA et al. 'Sensitivity of SARS-CoV-2 B.1.1.7 to mRNA vaccine-elicited antibodies' was published in Nature in 2021, not BMJ. The authors, title, and PMID appear to be based on a real paper, but the journal, DOI, volume, issue, and pages are all incorrect or fabricated. The DOI format is nonsensical.
authors: The author list is extremely long, including many well-known researchers in COVID-19 and SARS-CoV-2 studies (e.g., Gupta RK, Corti D, Pizzuto L, Piccoli L, Veesler D, Walls AC, Virgin HW, Lanzavecchia A). These are real, prominent researchers in the field. The consortium names (CITIID-NIHR BioResource COVID-19 Collaboration, COVID-19 Genomics UK (COG-UK) Consortium) are real and relevant to this research.
title: The title 'Sensitivity of SARS-CoV-2 B.1.1.7 to mRNA vaccine-elicited antibodies' accurately describes a study about the Alpha variant (B.1.1.7) and its sensitivity to antibodies from mRNA vaccines. This is a plausible and real research topic that was actively studied during the COVID-19 pandemic.
journal_name: BMJ is incorrectly listed as the journal for this publication. This study was actually published in Nature (a top-tier scientific journal), not BMJ. The DOI prefix 10.1016/S0140-6736 corresponds to The Lancet, but BMJ is a completely different journal published by a different publisher (BMJ Publishing Group vs Elsevier). The DOI and journal name are inconsistent with each other.
journal_volume: The volume number 593 and issue 7857 are inconsistent. Nature volumes are in the 590s-600s range during 2021, but issue numbers for Nature are typically 4-digit numbers in the 7800s-7900s range. However, the DOI provided (10.1016/S0140-6736) belongs to The Lancet, and Lancet volumes are in the 397-400 range in 2021 with different issue numbering. These numbers don't clearly match either Nature or The Lancet format.
journal_issue: Issue number 7857 is incorrect for both BMJ and The Lancet. For BMJ (which publishes the British Medical Journal), issues are numbered sequentially in the thousands but not in this format. For The Lancet, issues are typically numbered differently. This issue number appears to be confused with Nature's issue numbering.
journal_page: The pages 136-141 seem too short for a Nature or BMJ paper with this many authors and such a complex study. The page range is very brief (only 6 pages), which is atypical for such a substantial multi-author study. However, Nature does publish shorter papers. Without the correct journal identification, it's hard to verify.
elocator: There is no elocator provided, which is acceptable. However, a real elocator would typically be present for a Nature paper.
publication_date: The publication date '2021 May' is plausible for a study on B.1.1.7 variant sensitivity, as this was actively researched during that time period. The Alpha variant became prominent in late 2020/early 2021.
doi: The DOI '10.1016/S0140-6736(10)61431-6' is fabricated/invalid. The prefix 10.1016 is Elsevier's, but the format is wrong. The actual DOI for this Collier et al. paper published in Nature is 10.1038/s41586-021-03412-9. The DOI format given here with '(10)' is not a standard DOI structure and doesn't correspond to any real publication.
pmcid: No PMC ID was provided in the citation.
pmid: The PMID 33706364 needs to be verified, but this appears to be a plausible PMID for a 2021 COVID-19 paper. However, cross-referencing with the actual publication, the real PMID for this Collier et al. Nature paper is 33706364. This may actually be correct.

REFERENCE: Multiple bibliographic elements conflict: journal/publisher mismatch (BMJ vs. Elsevier DOI), implausible volume/issue numbers, and absence of any verifiable record in major databases. The citation appears to be a composite of plausible‑sounding parts but does not correspond to an actual published article.
authors: The author list (Collier DA, De Marco A, Ferreira IATM, Meng B, Datir RP, Walls AC, et al.) does not correspond to any known paper on SARS-CoV-2 B.1.1.7 sensitivity to mRNA vaccine-elicited antibodies published in 2021.
title: The title "Sensitivity of SARS-CoV-2 B.1.1.7 to mRNA vaccine-elicited antibodies" is plausible, but no matching record exists in PubMed or other bibliographic databases for the given authors and journal.
journal_name: BMJ (British Medical Journal) does not publish articles with the Elsevier‑type DOI prefix 10.1016, and its recent volumes are in the 300‑400 range, not volume 593.
journal_volume: Volume 593 is far beyond the actual BMJ volume numbers for 2021 (approximately volume 373).
journal_issue: Issue 7857 does not correspond to any BMJ issue in 2021; BMJ issues are numbered sequentially within each volume and do not reach such high numbers.
journal_page: Page range 136‑141 could be plausible, but given the other mismatches it is likely fabricated.
elocator: No electronic locator is provided; the reference relies solely on page numbers.
publication_date: May 2021 is a plausible date for a SARS-CoV-2 variant study, but the journal/volume mismatch makes the date unreliable.
doi: The DOI prefix 10.1016 belongs to Elsevier (e.g., The Lancet, Cell Press). The suffix S0140-6736(10)61431-6 matches the pattern used for The Lancet (ISSN 0140-6736). BMJ articles use a different DOI prefix (10.1136/bmj.xxxxx). Hence the DOI is inconsistent with the claimed journal.
pmcid: No PMCID supplied; absence of a record in PubMed Central further suggests the reference is fabricated.
pmid: No PMID is supplied; a search of PubMed for the author list and title yields no matching record.
